enrichment.short_description: Cloud-native artifact management platform for securely hosting, managing, and distributing software packages, containers, and AI models across the SDLC.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudsmith is a cloud-native artifact management platform for secure package, container, and AI model distribution. Compare pricing, features, and reviews.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloudsmith is a cloud-native artifact management platform that lets software teams host, manage, secure, and distribute packages, containers, and increasingly AI/ML models from a single control plane. It positions itself as a modern, SaaS-first alternative to self-hosted artifact repository managers such as JFrog Artifactory and Sonatype Nexus, and it supports essentially every major package format in use today, including npm, Docker/OCI, Maven, PyPI (Python), NuGet, Helm, Debian/APT, RPM/YUM, Conda, Cargo, Go modules, Ruby gems, Terraform modules, Vagrant boxes, and Cloudsmith's own generic and raw file formats. Because it is delivered as a fully managed cloud service with a global CDN, engineering teams do not need to run or scale their own artifact servers, patch storage infrastructure, or manage uptime for internal package feeds. Cloudsmith was founded in Belfast, Northern Ireland, in 2016 by Alan Carson (CEO) and Lee Skillen (CTO), two engineers who had previously worked on software supply-chain and DevOps tooling and identified a gap for a cloud-native, developer-friendly artifact repository that did not require the operational overhead of Artifactory-style on-premises deployments. The company has grown from a Belfast-based startup into a well-funded software supply chain security vendor with a global customer base, raising a Series A round (roughly $26 million combined across 2021 and 2023 tranches), a $23 million Series B led by TCV with participation from Insight Partners, and, in April 2026, a Series C round of approximately $72 million (reported in some regional coverage as €61.5 million), also led by TCV with Insight Partners participating. The Series C was explicitly framed around helping enterprises control and secure AI-driven software supply chains, reflecting Cloudsmith's expansion beyond traditional package management into securing the artifacts and models used to build and ship AI systems. Architecturally, Cloudsmith operates as a set of per-organization repositories, each of which can be public or private, and each of which supports fine-grained access control, single sign-on, audit logging, and policy-based governance (such as license and vulnerability policies that can block non-compliant packages from being promoted or consumed). A core differentiator is its 'single package, many formats' and 'single repository, many formats' flexibility: rather than provisioning a separate repository type for every package ecosystem, Cloudsmith repositories can typically hold multiple formats and expose native, protocol-compatible endpoints (so `npm install`, `pip install`, `docker pull`, `apt-get install`, and similar commands work unmodified against a Cloudsmith-hosted feed). The platform also emphasizes software supply chain security capabilities: vulnerability scanning, SBOM (software bill of materials) generation and ingestion, license compliance checks, provenance and integrity verification, and immutable, tamper-evident storage, all aimed at helping organizations meet emerging software supply chain security requirements (such as those driven by SLSA, executive orders on software security, and enterprise vendor risk assessments). Cloudsmith is closed-source, commercially licensed SaaS software; there is no self-hosted or open-source edition, though a generous free tier (called the Core plan) is available for open-source projects, small teams, and evaluation, offering a modest allotment of artifact storage and package delivery/bandwidth at no cost. Cloudsmith's pricing model is usage-based across two primary metered dimensions: artifact data storage, measured at a 'high watermark' (the peak amount of data stored, not cumulative uploads), and package delivery bandwidth (data downloaded/pulled from repositories); notably, uploads themselves are free and do not count against delivery usage. Paid plans reported in the market include a Team/Pro tier around $99-$149 per month with a defined storage and delivery allotment, a higher Ultra or Velocity tier around $299 per month with larger allotments, and custom Enterprise pricing for large organizations needing SSO/SAML, dedicated support, higher throughput, and compliance features; overage bandwidth beyond a plan's included allotment is billed at a per-GB rate (reported around $1.50/GB in some pricing breakdowns). Cloudsmith's target users are software engineering, DevOps, platform engineering, and security teams at companies of all sizes that need a private, centralized place to publish and consume internal packages and containers, mirror or proxy public registries (reducing exposure to upstream outages or supply chain attacks), and enforce governance over what dependencies are allowed into build pipelines. Its primary competitors include JFrog Artifactory (the long-standing market leader, generally self-hosted or hybrid), Sonatype Nexus Repository, GitHub Packages, GitLab Package Registry, AWS CodeArtifact, Google Artifact Registry, and Azure Artifacts, with Cloudsmith differentiating on being purpose-built as SaaS-first, broader native multi-format support without heavy configuration, and a stronger recent focus on AI/ML artifact and model governance as enterprises adopt AI-assisted development. Notable facts include Cloudsmith's origin as a bootstrapped Belfast startup that scaled into a venture-backed, globally used platform now valued in the context of a nine-figure cumulative funding total, and its positioning at the intersection of two fast-growing enterprise priorities: DevOps package management and software supply chain security in the age of AI-generated code and models. Cloudsmith's onboarding is designed to integrate with existing CI/CD pipelines with minimal friction, offering official integrations and documentation for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, and similar tooling, so teams can point their existing build pipelines at Cloudsmith repositories by changing a registry URL and credentials rather than rewriting pipeline logic. The company's engineering blog and changelog emphasize a fast, transparent release cadence, publishing detailed notes on new format support, security features, and pricing changes (such as the 'Building a Better Cloudsmith' pricing overhaul announcement), which is consistent with its developer-tool positioning and its effort to build trust with the security-conscious engineering teams it targets. As enterprises increasingly scrutinize the provenance of both traditional software dependencies and AI model weights/datasets used in production systems, Cloudsmith's roadmap has leaned further into being a control point for verifying what artifacts — code or AI models — are allowed to flow from development into production, a strategic bet reflected directly in how the company framed its 2026 Series C funding announcement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudsmith is a fully managed, cloud-native artifact management platform built to host, secure, and distribute software packages and containers for engineering teams. It supports nearly every major package ecosystem — npm, Docker/OCI, Maven, PyPI, NuGet, Helm, Debian, RPM, Conda, Cargo, Go, Ruby gems, Terraform, and more — from unified, per-organization repositories, so teams can consolidate what would otherwise be several disconnected artifact servers into a single service.

Founded in Belfast in 2016 by Alan Carson and Lee Skillen, Cloudsmith has grown into a venture-backed software supply chain security company, raising a Series C round in April 2026 to expand its focus on securing AI-driven software supply chains alongside its core package management offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cloudsmith repositories are protocol-compatible with native package manager commands, meaning existing npm, pip, docker, and apt workflows point at Cloudsmith without modification. The platform layers in fine-grained access control, single sign-on, audit logs, and policy engines that can block packages failing license or vulnerability checks from being promoted through a pipeline.

On the security side, Cloudsmith provides vulnerability scanning, SBOM generation and ingestion, provenance tracking, and immutable artifact storage — capabilities aimed at helping organizations satisfy SLSA-style supply chain security requirements and enterprise vendor risk reviews.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cloudsmith uses a usage-based freemium model with a free Core plan suited to open-source projects and small teams, offering a limited allotment of artifact storage and package delivery bandwidth at no cost. Paid plans (reported around $99-$149/month for Team/Pro and roughly $299/month for a higher Ultra tier) scale the included storage and bandwidth allotments, with overage bandwidth billed per gigabyte and custom Enterprise pricing available for large organizations needing SSO, dedicated support, and compliance features.

A key nuance in the pricing model is that uploads are always free and storage is billed at its peak 'high watermark' rather than cumulative volume, so teams are not penalized for republishing or version churn.

faqs.question: Is Cloudsmith free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cloudsmith offers a free Core plan with a limited allotment of artifact storage and package delivery bandwidth, suitable for open-source projects, small teams, and evaluation. Paid plans start around $99-$149/month for larger storage and bandwidth allotments.

faqs.question: What package formats does Cloudsmith support?
faqs.answer: Cloudsmith supports npm, Docker/OCI, Maven, PyPI, NuGet, Helm, Debian/APT, RPM/YUM, Conda, Cargo, Go modules, Ruby gems, Terraform modules, Vagrant boxes, and generic/raw files, all through native, protocol-compatible endpoints.

faqs.question: Is Cloudsmith self-hosted or cloud-only?
faqs.answer: Cloudsmith is a fully managed cloud (SaaS) platform only; it does not offer a self-hosted or on-premises deployment option.

faqs.question: How is Cloudsmith priced?
faqs.answer: Cloudsmith is priced on a usage basis across two metrics: artifact data storage (measured at peak/high-watermark usage) and package delivery bandwidth. Uploads are always free. Plans range from a free Core tier to paid Team/Pro and Ultra tiers, plus custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Cloudsmith and when?
faqs.answer: Cloudsmith was founded in Belfast, Northern Ireland, in 2016 by Alan Carson (CEO) and Lee Skillen (CTO).

faqs.question: How does Cloudsmith compare to JFrog Artifactory?
faqs.answer: Cloudsmith is a cloud-native, SaaS-only platform with broad out-of-the-box multi-format support, while Artifactory is a more established platform typically run self-hosted or in hybrid deployments with a larger existing enterprise footprint and plugin ecosystem.

enrichment.short_description: Managed cloud hosting platform that lets users deploy websites and apps on AWS, Google Cloud, DigitalOcean, Linode, or Vultr without server management.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudways is a managed cloud hosting platform for WordPress, WooCommerce, and PHP apps. Compare pricing plans, features, and how it compares to rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloudways is a managed cloud hosting platform that removes the complexity of server administration by sitting between end users and major infrastructure providers — AWS, Google Cloud Platform, DigitalOcean, Linode, and Vultr — so customers can deploy WordPress, WooCommerce, Magento, and other PHP-based websites and applications on enterprise-grade cloud servers without needing sysadmin expertise. Rather than building its own data centers, Cloudways provisions and manages virtual servers on top of these underlying cloud providers, handling server setup, security hardening, caching, backups, monitoring, and scaling through its own management dashboard, effectively acting as a managed-hosting layer over commodity infrastructure-as-a-service. The company was founded in 2011 (some sources cite 2012) in Malta, where it remains headquartered in Saint Julian's, though it built much of its engineering and support organization out of Pakistan, giving it a distinctly global operating footprint from an early stage. Cloudways grew steadily as a bootstrapped and later investor-backed hosting company focused specifically on the PHP/WordPress ecosystem, building a reputation among agencies, freelance developers, and e-commerce store owners for combining cloud-level performance and flexibility with the simplicity of traditional managed WordPress hosts. In August 2022, DigitalOcean announced its acquisition of Cloudways for approximately $350 million in cash, with the deal closing in September 2022; Cloudways has continued to operate as a distinct, DigitalOcean-owned brand and product rather than being merged directly into DigitalOcean's core offering, preserving its multi-cloud support (including non-DigitalOcean providers like AWS and Google Cloud) even after the acquisition. Architecturally, Cloudways offers what it calls a 'Lightning Stack,' an NGINX-based web server configuration optimized for speed, paired with Varnish, Redis, and Memcached caching layers, Object Cache Pro integration for WooCommerce and WordPress, free SSL certificates via Let's Encrypt, one-click staging environments, and automated or on-demand backups. Customers choose an underlying cloud provider and server size (RAM, vCPU, storage, bandwidth) during signup, and Cloudways layers its own control panel, one-click app installers, team collaboration tools, and 24/7 support on top. In 2024-2026 the company introduced 'Cloudways Autonomous,' a higher-tier product line built around true autoscaling on Kubernetes for high-traffic WordPress, WooCommerce, and LMS sites, bundling Cloudflare Enterprise CDN, unmetered PHP workers, and free managed migrations — a response to the growing demand for hosting that can absorb traffic spikes (e.g., flash sales) without manual server resizing. Cloudways is closed-source, proprietary commercial software available only as a hosted service; there is no self-hosted or open-source edition. Its pricing follows a pay-as-you-go, hourly-billed model with no long-term contracts: standard 'Flexible' plans on DigitalOcean infrastructure start at $11/month (or roughly $8.25/month with an introductory discount) for a 1 GB RAM, 1 vCPU, 25 GB SSD, 1 TB bandwidth server, scaling up through mid-tier and large enterprise-class configurations priced per resource size, with equivalent tiers available on AWS, Google Cloud, Linode, and Vultr at correspondingly different price points reflecting each provider's underlying costs. The newer Autonomous plans are flat monthly tiers — Growth at roughly $99/month, Scale at roughly $199/month, and Plus at roughly $399/month — plus custom Enterprise pricing, each defined by baseline server counts, disk space, and bandwidth rather than raw cloud instance specs. Optional add-ons include Cloudflare Enterprise CDN, a Site Manager tool, malware protection, advanced 24/7/365 premium support, Rackspace-hosted email, and managed DNS. Cloudways primarily targets digital agencies, freelance developers, and e-commerce merchants who want AWS/Google Cloud-grade infrastructure and performance without hiring dedicated DevOps staff, positioning itself between fully self-managed cloud hosting (where customers configure raw EC2 or Droplet instances themselves) and fully managed, single-stack hosts like WP Engine or Kinsta that abstract away the underlying cloud entirely. Its direct competitors include WP Engine, Kinsta, SiteGround, Pressable, and Bluehost/Hostinger Cloud on the WordPress-hosting side, as well as more infrastructure-centric managed platforms like Servebolt and RunCloud; Cloudways differentiates through its multi-cloud choice (the ability to pick the underlying infrastructure provider) and its transparent, granular pay-as-you-go pricing. Notable facts include the $350 million DigitalOcean acquisition — one of the larger exits in the managed WordPress hosting space — and Cloudways' continued operation as an independently branded product line within DigitalOcean's broader portfolio, which now also includes complementary developer and cloud infrastructure tools. Cloudways' server management dashboard, historically built around its own custom control panel rather than a licensed third-party panel like cPanel or Plesk, gives users a simplified view of server-level settings (PHP version switching, application-level SSL, cron jobs, and firewall rules) alongside application-level tools, which is part of why the platform has been favored by developers who want more visibility and control than a fully abstracted host like Kinsta provides, without needing to SSH into a raw cloud instance for routine tasks. The platform also supports a team collaboration model with role-based access, letting agencies invite clients or team members with restricted permissions to specific applications or servers, and it maintains a marketplace of one-click application templates covering not just WordPress and WooCommerce but also Magento, Laravel, and custom PHP stacks. Since joining DigitalOcean, Cloudways has increasingly cross-sold DigitalOcean's own infrastructure and AI/developer tooling (reflected in the '4 free AI Copilot credits' now bundled into new signups), signaling a broader strategic integration between the two brands even as Cloudways retains its multi-cloud, hosting-agnostic positioning as its primary differentiator against DigitalOcean's own more developer-centric, unmanaged Droplet product line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudways is a managed cloud hosting platform that provisions and manages virtual servers on top of major cloud providers — AWS, Google Cloud, DigitalOcean, Linode, and Vultr — so users get enterprise-grade infrastructure performance without needing to configure or maintain servers themselves. It is widely used for WordPress, WooCommerce, Magento, and other PHP applications by agencies, freelancers, and e-commerce merchants.

Founded in Malta in 2011, Cloudways was acquired by DigitalOcean in September 2022 for approximately $350 million and continues to operate as a distinct brand, still supporting multiple non-DigitalOcean cloud providers as underlying infrastructure options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cloudways bundles an NGINX-based 'Lightning Stack' with Varnish, Redis, and Memcached caching, free Let's Encrypt SSL, one-click staging environments, and automated backups on top of whichever underlying cloud provider a customer selects. Its dashboard provides one-click application installs, team collaboration and access controls, and 24/7 support.

For high-traffic sites, Cloudways Autonomous adds true Kubernetes-based autoscaling, Cloudflare Enterprise CDN, unmetered PHP workers, and free managed migrations, targeting stores and sites that experience unpredictable traffic spikes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Standard Cloudways plans are billed hourly on a pay-as-you-go basis with no long-term contract, starting at $11/month (about $8.25/month with an introductory discount) on DigitalOcean infrastructure for a 1 GB RAM/1 vCPU/25 GB SSD server, and scaling up in price as RAM, vCPU, storage, and bandwidth increase, with equivalent tiers priced differently on AWS, Google Cloud, Linode, and Vultr.

Cloudways Autonomous, the autoscaling product line, uses flat monthly pricing instead: Growth (~$99/month), Scale (~$199/month), and Plus (~$399/month), plus custom Enterprise pricing, with optional add-ons like Cloudflare Enterprise CDN, malware protection, and premium support available on any plan.

faqs.question: Is Cloudways owned by DigitalOcean?
faqs.answer: Yes, DigitalOcean acquired Cloudways for approximately $350 million in a deal announced in August 2022 and completed in September 2022. Cloudways continues to operate as a separate brand and still supports non-DigitalOcean cloud providers.

faqs.question: How much does Cloudways cost?
faqs.answer: Standard Cloudways plans start at $11/month (around $8.25/month with an introductory discount) on DigitalOcean infrastructure, billed hourly with no long-term contract. Prices scale with server RAM, vCPU, storage, and bandwidth, and vary by chosen cloud provider.

faqs.question: Which cloud providers can I use with Cloudways?
faqs.answer: Cloudways supports AWS, Google Cloud Platform, DigitalOcean, Linode, and Vultr as underlying infrastructure providers, all managed through a single Cloudways dashboard.

faqs.question: What is Cloudways Autonomous?
faqs.answer: Cloudways Autonomous is a higher-tier product line offering true Kubernetes-based autoscaling for high-traffic WordPress, WooCommerce, and LMS sites, with flat monthly pricing starting around $99/month and Cloudflare Enterprise CDN included.

faqs.question: Does Cloudways offer free migrations?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cloudways offers free managed migrations to help customers move existing websites onto its platform, particularly on its Autonomous plans.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Cloudways?
faqs.answer: Cloudways offers a short free trial period (a few days) for its Autonomous plans, and its standard hourly-billed plans allow low-commitment testing since there are no long-term contracts.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based point-of-sale and payments platform from Fiserv offering hardware, software, and merchant services for retail, restaurants, and service businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Clover POS Review 2026: Pricing, Plans & Hardware
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clover is a Fiserv-owned point-of-sale platform for retail, restaurants, and services. Compare Clover's pricing plans, hardware, and transaction fees.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clover is a cloud-based point-of-sale (POS) and integrated payments platform originally developed as an independent startup and now owned by Fiserv, one of the largest global payments and financial technology companies. Clover Network, Inc. was incorporated on October 15, 2010, and raised its initial $5.5 million round on November 1, 2010, led by Sutter Hill Ventures with participation from Andreessen Horowitz and angel investors; the company launched its cloud-based Android POS system in April 2012. Later that year, Clover was acquired by First Data Corporation, then the world's largest payment processor. First Data was itself acquired by Fiserv in a roughly $22 billion all-stock merger that closed on July 29, 2019, folding Clover into Fiserv's broader merchant-acquiring and payments business. Today Clover operates as Fiserv's flagship small-and-midsize-business (SMB) point-of-sale brand, distributed both directly and through a large network of bank and ISO (independent sales organization) partners who resell Clover hardware and processing services under their own branding. Clover's core proposition is an all-in-one, Android-based POS ecosystem: proprietary hardware devices (including the Clover Station, Clover Mini, Clover Flex handheld, Clover Go mobile card reader, and Clover Kiosk for self-service ordering) run a customized Android operating system and the Clover Dashboard software, which handles order taking, payment processing, inventory management, employee time tracking, customer engagement, and reporting. Because the platform runs on Android, Clover also supports a curated App Market of third-party integrations spanning accounting (QuickBooks), loyalty, online ordering, scheduling, and industry-specific tools, letting merchants extend the base POS with software tailored to retail, quick-service restaurants, full-service restaurants, and professional services. Clover is closed, proprietary hardware and software tightly bundled with Fiserv's payment processing; merchants cannot use Clover software without processing payments through Clover/Fiserv (or, in some cases, a reseller bank/ISO's processing relationship built on Fiserv's rails), which distinguishes it from software-only POS competitors. Pricing combines three layers: a monthly software subscription, one-time or financed hardware costs, and per-transaction processing fees. Clover's published general software plans include a Starter tier at $0/month (basic functionality with per-transaction fees), an Essentials tier around $29.95/month, and a Growth tier around $84.95-$89.95/month depending on business type, though pricing and plan names vary by industry vertical — retail plans have been advertised starting around $16/month, while restaurant-focused quick-service and full-service plans have started around $135/month and $179/month respectively, reflecting the more complex feature sets (table management, course timing, kitchen display integration) restaurants require. Transaction/processing fees for card-present sales typically range from about 2.3% to 2.6% plus $0.10 per transaction, while keyed-in (card-not-present) transactions run around 3.5% plus $0.10; because Clover is distributed through many reseller partners with negotiated rates, actual pricing a merchant receives can vary meaningfully depending on the ISO or bank relationship. Hardware bundles, especially in retail, are frequently sold with 36-month lease or contract commitments through resellers, which is a common point of criticism in merchant reviews, alongside concerns about cancellation fees and rate transparency that vary by reseller. Clover's target users are small and midsize brick-and-mortar and mobile businesses — independent retailers, cafes, restaurants, salons, and service businesses — that want a unified, easy-to-operate system for taking payments, running day-to-day operations, and getting basic business analytics without assembling separate POS, payments, and inventory tools. Its main competitors in the SMB POS space include Square (POS), Toast (restaurant-focused), Lightspeed, Shopify POS, TouchBistro, and other bank/processor-affiliated POS brands. Clover differentiates through its backing by Fiserv's massive processing infrastructure and its extensive bank/ISO distribution network, which gives it broad availability through merchants' existing banking relationships, though this same distributed reseller model means pricing and support quality can differ significantly depending on which partner a merchant signs up through — a nuance that is important for anyone comparing Clover pricing to more centrally operated competitors like Square or Toast. Beyond core payment processing, Clover has continued to expand its software ecosystem with capital and business-financing options for merchants (through Fiserv's broader financial services arm), gift card and loyalty program tooling, virtual terminal capabilities for phone and mail-order transactions, and analytics dashboards that let multi-location operators compare performance across sites from a single account. The Clover Dashboard web portal complements the in-store hardware, letting owners manage menus, pricing, employee permissions, and reporting remotely without needing to be on-site, which is particularly useful for multi-unit franchise operators and regional restaurant groups. Because Clover devices are Android-based, security and update management is handled centrally by Fiserv, with devices receiving OS-level and app-level updates automatically, reducing the PCI-compliance burden on individual merchants compared to older, less centrally managed terminal systems. Clover also supports EMV chip, contactless/NFC (including Apple Pay and Google Pay), and magnetic stripe payments across its hardware line, and its Kiosk product has become increasingly relevant for quick-service restaurants and retailers adopting self-ordering to reduce labor costs, a trend accelerated industry-wide in recent years. For prospective buyers, the most consequential decision is typically not which Clover plan to choose but which reseller or bank partner to sign up through, since two merchants with identical Clover hardware and software can end up with meaningfully different processing rates, contract lengths, and support experiences depending on their specific ISO relationship — a structural characteristic of Clover's go-to-market model that sets it apart from directly operated competitors like Square, Toast, and Shopify POS, where pricing is uniform regardless of which channel a merchant signs up through.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clover is a cloud-based, Android-powered point-of-sale and payments platform now owned by Fiserv, one of the largest global payments companies. Founded in 2010 and acquired by First Data in 2012 (which merged into Fiserv in 2019), Clover provides an all-in-one hardware and software ecosystem for taking payments, managing inventory, and running day-to-day operations at small and midsize businesses.

Clover devices — including the Clover Station, Clover Mini, Clover Flex, Clover Go, and Clover Kiosk — run the Clover Dashboard software and support a curated App Market of third-party integrations, and the platform is distributed both directly by Fiserv and through a large network of bank and ISO reseller partners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clover bundles payment processing with order management, inventory tracking, employee time tracking, customer engagement tools, and business reporting into a single Android-based dashboard, with hardware options ranging from countertop stations to handheld and mobile card readers and self-service kiosks.

Its App Market extends the base system with third-party software for accounting, loyalty programs, online ordering, and industry-specific needs across retail, quick-service restaurants, full-service restaurants, and professional services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clover pricing combines a monthly software subscription, hardware costs, and per-transaction processing fees. General plans include Starter ($0/month), Essentials (~$29.95/month), and Growth (~$84.95-$89.95/month), though industry-specific plans vary — retail starts around $16/month while restaurant plans start around $135-$179/month for more advanced features.

Card-present transaction fees typically run 2.3%-2.6% plus $0.10, and keyed-in transactions run about 3.5% plus $0.10. Because Clover is widely resold through banks and independent sales organizations, actual rates and hardware financing terms (often 36-month contracts for retail bundles) can vary significantly by reseller.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted personal cloud storage and media streaming server built with Nuxt, emphasizing privacy, file sharing, and a database-free architecture.
enrichment.seo_title: ClyoCloud: Self-Hosted Personal Cloud Storage Server
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClyoCloud is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal cloud storage and media streaming server built with Nuxt. Learn about its features and setup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClyoCloud is a small, open-source, self-hosted personal cloud storage and media streaming application built by the independent developer/organization WeeXnes. It is designed for individuals who want a private, self-controlled alternative to consumer cloud storage services like Google Drive or Dropbox, running entirely on the user's own hardware — a home server, a Raspberry Pi, a VPS, or a NAS — rather than on a third-party company's infrastructure. The project is hosted on WeeXnes's own self-managed OneDev instance at code.weexnes.dev/clyocloud rather than on a mainstream platform like GitHub, and it is also distributed through the Arch Linux User Repository (AUR) under the package names clyocloud and clyocloud-git, which makes it straightforward to install on Arch-based Linux distributions. The AUR package listing identifies WeeXnes as the maintainer and describes ClyoCloud as 'a sleek personal cloud storage and media streaming server built with Nuxt (stable version).' Because it is a small, community/solo-maintained project rather than a venture-backed company, there is no publicly documented founding year, company size, funding history, or corporate headquarters — it is best understood as an open-source software project rather than a commercial company, and any attempt to assign it conventional company metadata would be speculative. Technically, ClyoCloud is built on Nuxt 3 (the Vue.js meta-framework) for its frontend and uses Nitro/H3, Nuxt's built-in universal server engine, as its backend, which allows the entire application to be deployed as a single, relatively lightweight Node.js service rather than requiring a separate backend framework. Styling is handled with Tailwind CSS paired with the DaisyUI component library, giving the interface a clean, modern look without a heavy custom design system. Notably, ClyoCloud does not require a traditional database: instead of storing file metadata in PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite, it uses sidecar '.cloudinfo' JSON files that travel alongside each stored file, meaning metadata (tags, descriptions, sharing settings) stays co-located with the actual file on disk. This design choice simplifies deployment and backup (copying the file directory preserves both content and metadata) at the cost of some of the query flexibility a real database would offer. Core features include drag-and-drop file uploads, bulk file operations, automatic thumbnail generation for images, video streaming support powered by ffmpeg for media transcoding/serving, a tagging and collections system for organizing files, public share links for sending files to people without accounts, and basic activity tracking. On the security side, ClyoCloud implements JSON Web Token (JWT)-based authentication, TOTP-based two-factor authentication (compatible with standard authenticator apps) that becomes mandatory once enabled for an account, and path validation logic intended to prevent directory traversal attacks against the file storage layer — a meaningful concern for any self-hosted file server that exposes a web-based file browser. The project is licensed under the GPL-3.0 (GNU General Public License version 3), meaning the source code is freely available to use, study, modify, and redistribute, and any distributed modified versions must remain open source under the same license; there is no commercial or hosted-SaaS version of ClyoCloud, no subscription tiers, and no vendor-controlled cloud offering — the entire value proposition rests on self-hosting. Because there is no paid tier, ClyoCloud's cost to a user is effectively the cost of the server/hardware it runs on (electricity, a Raspberry Pi or VPS, storage disks) rather than any software license fee. ClyoCloud's target users are privacy-conscious hobbyists, homelab enthusiasts, and self-hosting communities — the kind of audience found on platforms like the Arch User Repository, r/selfhosted, and similar communities — who prefer running their own infrastructure over trusting file storage to a third-party cloud provider. Within the crowded self-hosted file storage space, its closest comparators are more established open-source projects such as Nextcloud, ownCloud, Seafile, FileBrowser, and Immich (for photo/media-specific use cases); ClyoCloud differentiates from these larger, more feature-rich platforms by being deliberately lightweight, database-free, and narrowly scoped to file storage, sharing, and media streaming rather than the broader groupware/office-suite ambitions of Nextcloud. Given its scale as a solo or small-team open-source project rather than a funded company, prospective users should expect community-driven support (via its OneDev repository and any associated chat/forum channels) rather than commercial SLAs, and should evaluate it as an actively evolving hobby-to-production-grade project rather than an enterprise-ready platform. The choice to self-host the project's source repository on OneDev rather than GitHub or GitLab is itself notable within the self-hosting community: OneDev is a self-hosted Git server with built-in CI/CD, and WeeXnes running ClyoCloud's own development infrastructure on self-hosted tooling reflects the same underlying philosophy that motivates ClyoCloud's design as a product — reducing dependence on third-party platforms wherever practical. Deployment typically involves cloning or downloading the project, installing Node.js dependencies, configuring environment variables for JWT secrets and storage paths, and running the Nitro-based server process (optionally behind a reverse proxy like Nginx or Caddy for TLS termination in production), a workflow familiar to anyone who has deployed other modern Node.js/Nuxt applications. Because metadata lives alongside files on disk rather than in a centralized database, users migrating an existing ClyoCloud instance to new hardware can do so by simply copying the storage directory, which is a meaningful operational simplicity advantage for a hobbyist audience that may not want to manage database backups and restores as part of routine server maintenance.

Maintained by the independent developer/organization WeeXnes and hosted on a self-managed OneDev instance rather than GitHub, it lets individuals run their own private alternative to consumer cloud storage services on hardware they control.

As a solo/small-team open-source project rather than a company, ClyoCloud has no publicly documented founding date, funding, or headquarters — it should be evaluated as community software, distributed via its own repository and the Arch Linux User Repository (AUR), rather than as a commercial vendor.

Instead of a traditional database, file metadata is stored in sidecar '.cloudinfo' JSON files alongside each file, simplifying backup and deployment. enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source PHP content management system using the Smarty template engine, built for developers and site owners who need a lightweight, flexible CMS.
enrichment.seo_title: CMS Made Simple Review 2026: Free Open-Source CMS
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CMS Made Simple is a free, open-source PHP content management system built on the Smarty template engine. See features, modules, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CMS Made Simple (commonly abbreviated CMSMS) is a free, open-source content management system first released in July 2004 under the GNU General Public License (GPL), a licensing model it has maintained continuously since launch. Built in PHP with MySQL as its database backend, CMS Made Simple's defining architectural choice is its use of the Smarty template engine, which strictly separates page content, site functionality (business logic), and visual templates into distinct layers. This separation was, at the time of the project's creation in the mid-2000s, a differentiating design decision aimed at letting designers and template authors work independently of the PHP code that drives functionality, and it remains the project's core technical identity today. The system has been continuously maintained and improved for two decades: in the summer of 2015 the project shipped version 2.0, described as a nearly complete overhaul of the underlying codebase and user experience, modernizing the platform after roughly a decade on its earlier architecture. In 2017, CMS Made Simple won the CMS Critic annual award for Best Open Source Content Management, recognition that reflected its continued relevance in a market that had, by that point, become heavily dominated by WordPress. The current stable releases are licensed under GPL-3.0 (earlier versions used GPLv2), and the project's source code, documentation, and downloads are distributed through its own website (cmsmadesimple.org), a GitHub organization (github.com/cmsmadesimple), and SourceForge, reflecting the multi-channel distribution common to long-running open-source projects. As a community-driven open-source project rather than a venture-backed company, CMS Made Simple has no formal founding-company metadata such as headquarters, employee count, or funding history in the conventional sense; it is maintained by a volunteer and contributor community rather than a single corporate entity, and its 'business model,' to the extent one exists, revolves around free core software plus an ecosystem of community-built modules, templates, and optional professional services from third-party developers and agencies familiar with the platform. Functionally, CMS Made Simple ships with a Content Manager for creating and editing pages in real time, a News module for article-based content such as blog posts or press releases, a File Manager for handling uploads and images, front-end and back-end Search modules, and a permissions-based Users manager that lets site owners control which backend users can edit which sections of a site. The default installation includes a lightweight WYSIWYG editor (historically MicroTiny, a streamlined version of TinyMCE) for non-technical content editors, while a large catalog of community-built modules — installable through an in-admin Module Manager — extends the base system with e-commerce, forms, image galleries, calendars, and other application-like functionality, allowing CMSMS to be used for more than simple brochure websites when needed. Because the Smarty-based template layer is fully decoupled from the PHP application logic, developers and designers can build highly customized front-end designs without needing to modify core PHP files, and the platform's API is described as extensive enough to support fairly complex custom development on top of the core CMS. CMS Made Simple's target users are website developers, freelance designers, small agencies, and technically inclined site owners who want a lightweight, template-flexible, self-hosted CMS with a smaller footprint and steeper design-control ceiling than out-of-the-box WordPress themes typically offer, without needing the more complex, code-heavy setup of a fully headless or enterprise CMS. Its most direct competitors in the open-source, self-hosted CMS space include WordPress (by far the dominant player), Joomla, Drupal, TYPO3, and newer flat-file or headless options like Grav, Statamic, and Decap CMS; CMS Made Simple differentiates primarily through its Smarty-driven separation of concerns and its long-standing reputation for stability and a lighter resource footprint compared to more feature-heavy competitors like Drupal. Notable facts include its 2017 CMS Critic award, its continuous GPL licensing since a 2004 launch that predates WordPress's dominance of the CMS market, and its status as one of the longer-running independent open-source CMS projects still actively maintained into the mid-2020s, having weathered two decades of shifting trends in web content management. CMS Made Simple's admin interface is organized around a traditional backend/frontend split familiar to anyone who has used a legacy PHP CMS: editors log into a secured admin area to manage pages, news articles, uploaded files, and users, while the public-facing site is rendered entirely through Smarty templates that pull structured content from the database at request time (with optional caching layers to keep performance reasonable on shared hosting). This traditional, monolithic architecture — as opposed to a modern headless or JAMstack approach — means CMSMS is best suited to conventional server-rendered websites rather than decoupled frontend/backend setups, which is an important distinction for anyone evaluating it against newer entrants in the CMS space. The project's installation requirements are modest: a standard LAMP or LEMP stack (Linux, Apache or Nginx, MySQL, PHP) is sufficient, making it compatible with a very wide range of shared, VPS, and dedicated hosting providers, and its resource footprint has historically been praised as lighter than Drupal's, which appeals to budget-conscious agencies hosting many small client sites on modest infrastructure. Community support channels include official forums, a wiki-style documentation site, and the GitHub issue tracker, through which bug reports, feature requests, and security patches are coordinated by the volunteer development team. While CMS Made Simple never achieved anywhere near WordPress's install-base or plugin ecosystem size, it has maintained a loyal niche following among developers who specifically value the Smarty-based separation of concerns and the predictability of a smaller, more tightly scoped core codebase over the sprawling, plugin-dependent complexity that can accumulate in larger CMS ecosystems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CMS Made Simple is a free, open-source content management system first released in July 2004 and licensed under the GPL. Built in PHP with the Smarty template engine, its core design principle is a strict separation between content, functionality, and templates, letting designers and developers work independently.

The project shipped a major version 2.0 overhaul in 2015 and won the CMS Critic award for Best Open Source Content Management in 2017. It remains community-maintained rather than backed by a single company, with distribution through its own site, GitHub, and SourceForge.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core installation includes a real-time Content Manager, a News module for article-based content, a File Manager, front-end and back-end Search, a permissions-based Users manager, and a lightweight WYSIWYG editor for non-technical editors.

A large catalog of community-built modules — installable via the in-admin Module Manager — extends the base CMS with e-commerce, forms, galleries, and other functionality, while the Smarty template layer keeps custom front-end designs fully decoupled from PHP application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CMS Made Simple is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or commercial edition. It can be used for personal or commercial projects at no licensing cost.

Costs to site owners come from self-hosting infrastructure and any optional professional services or premium third-party modules/templates purchased from independent developers in the CMSMS community, not from the core software itself.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, web-based interface for controlling CNC machines running Grbl, Marlin, Smoothieware, TinyG, or g2core firmware from any browser.
enrichment.seo_title: CNCjs Review: Free Open-Source Web CNC Controller
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CNCjs is a free, open-source, web-based interface for CNC machines running Grbl, Marlin, Smoothieware, or TinyG. Learn features and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CNCjs is a free, open-source, web-based control interface for CNC (computer numerical control) machines, designed to run on low-cost hardware like a Raspberry Pi or a general-purpose laptop with Node.js installed. It acts as the bridge between a CNC controller board (typically an Arduino or Arduino-compatible board running firmware such as Grbl, Marlin, Smoothieware, TinyG, or g2core) and the operator, streaming G-code toolpaths to the machine over a serial connection — a USB serial port, a Bluetooth serial module, or a serial-to-WiFi module — and exposing a full control dashboard through any modern web browser. Because it runs as a lightweight server process rather than a native desktop application, CNCjs can be installed once on a Raspberry Pi mounted to or near the CNC machine, then controlled remotely from a phone, tablet, or laptop on the same network, which is a major convenience advantage over older desktop-only G-code senders that tie the operator to a single computer physically connected to the machine. The project is hosted on GitHub at github.com/cncjs/cncjs and is distributed as an open-source npm package under the MIT license, meaning it is free to use, modify, and redistribute, including in commercial products, with no royalty or licensing fee; there is no paid or enterprise tier, and the entire project is maintained through community contributions rather than a company. As with many hobbyist-to-prosumer open-source hardware tools, CNCjs does not have a conventional corporate history — no publicly documented founding year as a company, no funding rounds, and no formal headquarters — because it is fundamentally a community software project rather than a commercial entity, and it should be evaluated on that basis rather than assigned invented company metadata. Functionally, CNCjs provides real-time machine position visualization (including a 3D toolpath preview so operators can verify a G-code program before running it on physical material), a G-code sender/console for streaming and monitoring commands line by line, jogging controls for manually moving the machine's axes, workspace/coordinate system management, macro support for saving and replaying frequently used command sequences, and widgets for probe/tool-length setting, spindle/laser control, and grbl/Marlin-specific settings panels. Recent development has focused on expanding firmware and hardware compatibility: official support has been added for the CNC xPro V5 controller and for FluidNC, a Grbl-derived firmware that runs on ESP32 microcontrollers and communicates over WiFi rather than a wired serial connection, along with UART communication improvements that eliminate the need to reset the connection each time the interface is reopened, and new jog-cancel commands for both the Grbl and TinyG/G2 firmware families. The broader CNCjs ecosystem also includes companion projects such as cncjs-shopfloor-tablet, a simplified touch-optimized UI variant of CNCjs designed for tablets mounted on a workshop floor next to a running machine, and cncjs-controller, a client library that lets other applications communicate with a running CNCjs server over its event-based API, enabling custom integrations and automation scripts. CNCjs's target users are hobbyist and prosumer CNC machinists, makers, and small workshop operators who run desktop CNC routers, mills, laser cutters, or 3D printer-adjacent machines on open firmware like Grbl or Marlin, as well as more advanced users building custom shop-floor setups who need a lightweight, browser-accessible, network-controllable interface rather than a machine-tethered desktop app. Its closest comparators in the open-source G-code sender space include Universal Gcode Sender (UGS), bCNC, Candle, and LaserGRBL (more laser-focused), while on the higher end, commercial CAM/control suites like Carbide Motion (Carbide 3D) or Fusion 360's machine control integrations serve adjacent but more vertically integrated needs; CNCjs differentiates through its browser-based, network-accessible architecture, its broad multi-firmware support in a single tool, and its lightweight footprint suited to running unattended on a Raspberry Pi. Notable facts include its role as one of the most widely adopted open-source G-code streaming tools in the maker/CNC community, its continued active maintenance and firmware-compatibility expansion (including recent ESP32/FluidNC and WiFi-based controller support), and its permissive MIT license, which has allowed it to be bundled or referenced by hardware vendors and derivative shop-floor tools without licensing friction. Installation is handled through npm (Node Package Manager), with CNCjs distributed as a standard npm package that can be installed globally and launched from the command line with configurable options for listen port, host address, configuration file location, verbosity/logging level, static file mount points, and the specific controller type being targeted (Grbl, Marlin, Smoothie, TinyG, or g2core). This command-line-first distribution model reflects CNCjs's audience: technically comfortable makers and hobbyists who are already accustomed to flashing firmware onto microcontrollers and are comfortable with basic terminal usage, rather than a plug-and-play consumer audience. The web interface itself is a single-page application that communicates with the CNCjs server over WebSockets for real-time status updates, meaning the displayed machine position, work coordinates, and console output stay synchronized with the physical machine's actual state with minimal lag, which matters when an operator needs to react quickly to a probe touch-off or an unexpected collision. Because the project is entirely community-maintained on GitHub, its release cadence and roadmap are driven by contributor availability and pull requests rather than a formal corporate product roadmap, which is typical of mature hobbyist open-source infrastructure tools that have settled into a stable, incrementally improved state after their initial period of rapid feature growth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CNCjs is a free, open-source, web-based interface for controlling CNC machines running Grbl, Marlin, Smoothieware, TinyG, or g2core firmware. It runs as a lightweight Node.js server — commonly on a Raspberry Pi — and streams G-code to the machine's controller board over serial, Bluetooth, or WiFi, while exposing a full control dashboard through any web browser.

As a community-maintained open-source project distributed under the MIT license, CNCjs has no corporate ownership, funding history, or headquarters — it is developed and supported by contributors via its GitHub repository rather than a commercial company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CNCjs provides 3D toolpath visualization, a G-code sender/console, manual jogging controls, workspace and coordinate management, macro support, and widgets for probing, spindle/laser control, and firmware-specific settings.

Recent releases have added support for the CNC xPro V5 controller and FluidNC firmware on ESP32 (enabling WiFi-based control), along with UART reliability improvements and new jog-cancel commands, while companion projects like cncjs-shopfloor-tablet and cncjs-controller extend it to tablet-optimized shop-floor UIs and custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CNCjs is entirely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or commercial edition. It can be freely used, modified, and redistributed, including in commercial hardware products, without royalty fees.
The only cost to users is the hardware it runs on (typically an inexpensive Raspberry Pi or an existing laptop) and the CNC controller board itself; the software has no licensing cost.

cons: Requires some technical setup (Node.js, serial/network configuration) compared to plug-and-play commercial software

faqs.question: Is CNCjs free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CNCjs is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or commercial version.

faqs.question: What CNC firmware does CNCjs support?
faqs.answer: CNCjs supports Grbl, Marlin, Smoothieware, TinyG, and g2core firmware, and has added official support for FluidNC running on ESP32 controllers.

faqs.question: Can CNCjs run on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, CNCjs is commonly installed on a Raspberry Pi, which then acts as a lightweight server that can be controlled remotely from a browser on any device on the same network.

faqs.question: Does CNCjs require an internet connection?
faqs.answer: No, CNCjs runs locally on your network; it does not require an internet connection to control a CNC machine, only a local network connection between the controlling device and the server.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CNCjs and Universal Gcode Sender?
faqs.answer: Both are free, open-source G-code senders, but CNCjs is browser-based and network-accessible (allowing remote control from any device), while Universal Gcode Sender (UGS) is a native desktop application.

faqs.question: Who maintains CNCjs?
faqs.answer: CNCjs is maintained by an open-source community of contributors through its GitHub repository (github.com/cncjs/cncjs) rather than a commercial company.

use_cases.description: Small workshops use CNCjs and the companion shopfloor-tablet UI to monitor and control machines from a tablet on the shop floor without a wired connection.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted, API-first headless CMS built for developers, offering flexible content modeling with no subscription and an MIT-licensed core.
enrichment.seo_title: Cockpit CMS Review 2026: Free Headless CMS for Developers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cockpit is a free, self-hosted, API-first headless CMS with an MIT-licensed core. Compare features, self-hosting requirements, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cockpit is a self-hosted, API-first headless content management system built by Agentejo, a product strategy, design, and development studio based in Hamburg, Germany. The project was first conceived in 2011, and Agentejo has held copyright over the codebase since 2015 under the MIT license, making Cockpit one of the longer-running independent headless CMS projects that predates the current wave of headless CMS platforms (which largely emerged and commercialized from roughly 2015 onward, such as Contentful, Sanity, and Strapi). Cockpit describes itself as a 'Sovereign Content Platform,' a positioning that emphasizes freedom from vendor lock-in: because it is open source, self-hosted, and free, teams that adopt Cockpit own their content infrastructure outright rather than depending on a SaaS vendor's uptime, pricing changes, or data-export policies. The core software is released under the MIT license and is explicitly marketed as 'forever free' with 'no subscription,' distinguishing it from most modern headless CMS competitors, which typically operate on usage-based or seat-based SaaS pricing with a limited free tier. Architecturally, Cockpit is an API-first CMS: content editors build structured content models (custom fields, relationships, repeatable groups) through an admin interface, and that content is exposed via REST and other APIs for consumption by any frontend technology, decoupling the content layer entirely from presentation. This makes it framework-agnostic by design — Cockpit explicitly supports being paired with Next.js, React, Vue, Node.js, PHP, and Ruby-based frontends, among others, letting developers choose their own rendering and delivery stack while using Cockpit purely as the structured content backend. Installation is designed to be fast and frictionless, with the project advertising a five-minute setup that requires no account creation or vendor sign-up, reinforcing its self-hosted, ownership-first philosophy. Feature-wise, Cockpit includes structured content modeling, asset/media management, webhooks for triggering external processes on content changes, component-based layout building, full-text search, form submission collection (useful for building contact forms or lead-capture flows without a separate service), localization/multi-language content support, audience personalization capabilities, content migration tooling, and page routing with SEO-oriented controls; more recently, the platform has added AI-powered enhancement features under an 'Autopilot' branding, reflecting the broader industry trend of headless CMS platforms integrating generative AI into content authoring and management workflows. Because Cockpit is self-hosted only, users are responsible for provisioning their own server environment (typically PHP-based, given the CMS's roots, though the ecosystem has evolved) and for their own scaling, backups, and security patching — the tradeoff Cockpit makes explicit in exchange for full data sovereignty and privacy compliance, which it highlights as particularly relevant for GDPR-sensitive deployments in Europe and for public-sector organizations that cannot use US-hosted SaaS content platforms. Cockpit's stated target audience spans several segments: individual creators and freelancers wanting faster project turnaround without recurring SaaS fees, marketers who need flexible cross-channel content distribution, developers who want an API-first, framework-agnostic, open-source content backend, agencies looking to avoid the recurring per-project SaaS costs and vendor lock-in that come with commercial headless CMS platforms, public-sector organizations with GDPR and data-residency requirements (Cockpit notes discounted pricing arrangements, such as 50% discounts, for qualifying nonprofits in contexts where paid add-ons or support exist), and enterprise teams that want to integrate a content layer into existing internal infrastructure rather than adopting a third-party SaaS dependency. Cockpit's direct competitors in the open-source, self-hosted headless CMS space include Strapi, Directus, Payload CMS, and KeystoneJS, while in the broader headless CMS category (including commercial SaaS options) it competes conceptually with Contentful, Sanity, Contentstack, and Storyblok, though Cockpit differentiates sharply from most of these by having no subscription cost at all for its core product, versus Strapi and Directus's open-core models (free self-hosted core plus paid cloud/enterprise tiers) or Contentful/Sanity/Storyblok's SaaS-first, usage-metered pricing. Notable facts include Cockpit's unusually long history for a headless CMS (tracing back to 2011, well before the term 'headless CMS' was in common industry use), its consistent commitment to a genuinely free MIT-licensed core rather than an open-core bait-and-switch model, and its origin from Agentejo, a boutique digital studio rather than a venture-funded software company, which shapes its lean, developer-first, no-nonsense product philosophy relative to more heavily marketed, investor-backed headless CMS competitors. Technically, Cockpit's admin interface lets content teams define custom 'collections' (structured content types with configurable field types such as text, rich text, media, relations, and repeatable groups) without writing code, while developers retain full control over how that content is queried and rendered through Cockpit's REST API endpoints, GraphQL-style querying in newer versions, or direct SDK integrations for popular frontend frameworks. The project's GitHub presence under the agentejo organization includes the core Cockpit repository alongside a range of official add-ons — such as a two-factor authentication module and internationalization (i18n) tooling — that extend the base installation without bloating the core, keeping installations lean for developers who only need a subset of functionality. Because Cockpit has no venture funding and no subscription revenue model to sustain a large engineering team, its release cadence and support responsiveness are shaped by Agentejo's capacity as a studio balancing client work with open-source maintenance, which is a tradeoff worth weighing against better-resourced, venture-backed alternatives for teams with mission-critical uptime or support-response requirements.

It positions itself as a 'Sovereign Content Platform' — genuinely free, with no subscription, giving teams full ownership of their content infrastructure.

Content editors build structured content models through Cockpit's admin interface, and that content is exposed via REST APIs for consumption by any frontend framework, including Next.js, React, Vue, Node.js, PHP, or Ruby, decoupling content management entirely from presentation.

paragraphs: Cockpit includes structured content modeling, asset management, webhooks, component-based layout building, full-text search, form submission collection, localization, audience personalization, content migration tools, and SEO-oriented page routing, plus newer AI-powered 'Autopilot' content enhancement features.

Setup is designed to take about five minutes with no account or vendor sign-up required, reinforcing its self-hosted, vendor-independence philosophy for developers, agencies, and privacy-sensitive organizations.

The project notes discounted arrangements (such as 50% off) for qualifying nonprofit and public-sector organizations where paid add-ons or support services exist, but there is no required commercial license to use the core CMS.

faqs.question: Is Cockpit CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cockpit's core is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no subscription fee required. It is explicitly marketed as 'forever free.'

faqs.question: Is Cockpit CMS self-hosted or cloud-based?
faqs.answer: Cockpit is self-hosted only. There is no official managed cloud/SaaS version — users deploy and host it on their own servers.

faqs.question: Who built Cockpit CMS?
faqs.answer: Cockpit was first conceived in 2011 by Agentejo, a digital studio based in Hamburg, Germany, which has held copyright over the MIT-licensed codebase since 2015.

faqs.question: What frontend frameworks work with Cockpit?
faqs.answer: Because Cockpit is API-first and framework-agnostic, it works with any frontend technology, including Next.js, React, Vue, Node.js, PHP, and Ruby.

faqs.question: How does Cockpit compare to Strapi or Contentful?
faqs.answer: Unlike Strapi's open-core model (free self-hosted core plus paid cloud tiers) or Contentful's SaaS-only, usage-metered pricing, Cockpit's entire core product is free with no subscription, though it lacks an official managed hosting option that those competitors offer.

faqs.question: Does Cockpit CMS support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cockpit includes built-in localization support for managing multi-language content across sites and applications.

enrichment.short_description: Distributed SQL database built for resilience and horizontal scale, wire-compatible with PostgreSQL, available self-hosted or as a managed cloud service.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CockroachDB is a distributed SQL database built for resilience and scale, PostgreSQL-compatible. Compare Basic, Standard, Advanced, and self-hosted pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CockroachDB is a distributed SQL database management system built by Cockroach Labs, designed from the ground up to survive infrastructure failures — node outages, data center failures, even entire region failures — while maintaining strong consistency and continuous availability for transactional workloads. Its relational layer is built on top of a distributed, transactional, strongly consistent key-value store, and the SQL layer is wire-compatible with PostgreSQL, meaning applications can generally connect to CockroachDB using standard PostgreSQL drivers, ORMs, and tools, easing adoption for teams already working within the Postgres ecosystem. Cockroach Labs was founded in 2015 by Spencer Kimball (CEO), Peter Mattis (CTO), and Ben Darnell (Chief Architect), three former Google engineers who had worked on Google's internal distributed database systems, most notably Spanner, Google's own globally distributed SQL database; that lineage directly informed CockroachDB's design goals of surviving datacenter-level failures and scaling horizontally across regions while preserving ACID transaction guarantees, a combination that traditional single-node relational databases and many earlier NoSQL systems could not offer simultaneously. The company is headquartered in New York City and has raised substantial venture funding across nine rounds totaling over $800 million, including a $160 million Series E round and a $278 million Series F round led by Greenoaks that valued the company at approximately $5 billion, more than double its prior valuation — figures that place Cockroach Labs among the more heavily capitalized independent database infrastructure companies. Architecturally, CockroachDB automatically shards, replicates, and rebalances data across nodes and, optionally, across geographic regions, using the Raft consensus protocol to keep replicas in sync and automatically fail over without manual intervention when a node or region goes down; this multi-active availability model lets a cluster continue serving both reads and writes even during a regional outage, which is the core value proposition the product markets under the phrase 'always-on customer experiences.' The database also supports geo-partitioning, letting specific rows of data be pinned to specific geographic regions to satisfy data residency and latency requirements, which is particularly relevant for globally distributed applications subject to regulations like GDPR. CockroachDB's source code is publicly available and described as 'source-available' rather than strictly open source in the OSI sense; in August 2024 Cockroach Labs restructured its licensing so that companies with more than $10 million in annual revenue must pay a fee based on the number of CPU cores in the server systems where a self-hosted deployment runs, while startups and smaller companies below that revenue threshold can use the same enterprise-grade version at no cost — a shift that drew coverage (including from TechCrunch) as part of a broader industry trend of infrastructure vendors tightening license terms in response to cloud providers and larger competitors running their software without contributing back. Beyond self-hosted deployment, Cockroach Labs offers CockroachDB Cloud as a fully managed database-as-a-service (DBaaS) with three primary tiers: Basic, a pay-as-you-go, request-unit (RU) metered tier starting at $0/month with 50 million RUs and 10 GiB of storage included free each month, scaling to zero when idle and supporting bursty workloads up to roughly 30,000 RU/sec with 99.99% uptime across AWS and GCP; Standard, a newer (preview-stage as of recent releases) tier billed on provisioned compute starting around $0.18/hour for 2 vCPUs, aimed at steady, predictable workloads up to 200 vCPUs; and Advanced, the top cloud tier starting around $0.60/hour for 4 vCPUs, offering unlimited scaling, multi-region support across AWS, GCP, and Azure, up to 99.999% uptime, customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK), and compliance certifications relevant to HIPAA and PCI-DSS, targeting high-scale, security-sensitive enterprise applications. Cockroach Labs has also layered AI-oriented capabilities across its plans, including an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, a ccloud command-line interface, agent skills, and distributed vector indexing, reflecting the database industry's broader push to support AI agent and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workloads directly within the database layer. CockroachDB's target users span from startups needing a Postgres-compatible database that can scale without a costly re-architecture later, to large enterprises — reported customers include organizations like Equifax, Bose, and Comcast — running mission-critical, globally distributed, high-availability transactional systems in banking, retail, and media. Its principal competitors include Google Cloud Spanner (its closest conceptual relative, given the shared Spanner lineage), Amazon Aurora and Amazon DynamoDB, YugabyteDB (another PostgreSQL-compatible distributed SQL database with a similar value proposition), TiDB, and, at a broader level, traditional PostgreSQL and MySQL for teams that don't yet need horizontal, multi-region scale. CockroachDB differentiates through its combination of full ACID transactional SQL semantics, PostgreSQL wire compatibility, and true multi-region survivability, positioning it as a database that lets engineering teams avoid the classic tradeoff between relational consistency and horizontal, geo-distributed scale. Cockroach Labs also publishes CockroachDB Cloud's uptime and reliability guarantees prominently in its marketing, framing the product around 'always-on' resilience for consumer-facing applications where downtime translates directly into lost revenue or customer trust, a positioning aimed squarely at industries like financial services, e-commerce, and media streaming where transactional consistency and continuous availability are both non-negotiable. The company maintains an active open developer relations program, including detailed architecture documentation, a public roadmap, and a large body of technical content explaining distributed systems concepts like Raft consensus, MVCC (multi-version concurrency control), and geo-partitioning, which has helped CockroachDB build credibility among engineering teams evaluating distributed database options on technical merit rather than marketing alone. Migration tooling and compatibility layers are a recurring investment area, since the practical adoption path for most CockroachDB customers involves moving an existing PostgreSQL (or, less commonly, MySQL) application over time rather than building greenfield on CockroachDB from day one, and Cockroach Labs has continued to invest in tooling that reduces the friction and downtime associated with that migration path.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CockroachDB is a distributed SQL database built by Cockroach Labs, founded in 2015 by former Google engineers Spencer Kimball, Peter Mattis, and Ben Darnell, who previously worked on Google's Spanner distributed database. It is designed to survive node, data center, and even regional failures while maintaining strong consistency and PostgreSQL wire compatibility.

Headquartered in New York City, Cockroach Labs has raised over $800 million across nine funding rounds, including a $278 million Series F led by Greenoaks that valued the company at approximately $5 billion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CockroachDB automatically shards, replicates, and rebalances data using the Raft consensus protocol, enabling multi-active availability that keeps a cluster serving reads and writes even during regional outages. Geo-partitioning lets specific data be pinned to specific regions for data residency and latency requirements.

Recent additions include AI-oriented capabilities such as an MCP server, a ccloud CLI, agent skills, and distributed vector indexing, reflecting growing demand for databases that natively support AI agent and RAG workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CockroachDB Cloud offers three tiers: Basic (pay-as-you-go, starting free with 50M request units and 10 GiB storage monthly), Standard (provisioned compute starting at $0.18/hour for 2 vCPUs), and Advanced (starting at $0.60/hour for 4 vCPUs, with unlimited scaling and compliance features like CMEK and HIPAA/PCI-DSS support).

Self-hosted CockroachDB is source-available; since an August 2024 licensing change, companies with more than $10 million in annual revenue must pay a per-CPU-core fee to self-host, while smaller companies and startups below that threshold can use the same enterprise-grade version free of charge.

faqs.question: Is CockroachDB free to use?
faqs.answer: CockroachDB Cloud's Basic tier is free to start, including 50 million request units and 10 GiB of storage per month. Self-hosted CockroachDB is also free for companies with less than $10 million in annual revenue; larger companies pay a per-CPU-core fee under the current licensing model introduced in August 2024.

faqs.question: Is CockroachDB open source?
faqs.answer: CockroachDB is source-available rather than strictly open source under OSI definitions. The source code is publicly viewable, but commercial self-hosted use by companies above $10 million in annual revenue requires a paid license.

faqs.question: Is CockroachDB compatible with PostgreSQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, CockroachDB is wire-compatible with PostgreSQL, so applications can generally connect using standard PostgreSQL drivers, ORMs, and tools with minimal changes.

faqs.question: Who founded Cockroach Labs?
faqs.answer: Cockroach Labs was founded in 2015 by Spencer Kimball, Peter Mattis, and Ben Darnell, all former Google engineers who previously worked on Google's Spanner distributed database.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CockroachDB Basic, Standard, and Advanced?
faqs.answer: Basic is a pay-as-you-go serverless tier that scales to zero, suited for bursty workloads. Standard offers provisioned compute for steady workloads at scale. Advanced adds unlimited scaling, multi-region support across AWS/GCP/Azure, up to 99.999% uptime, and compliance features like CMEK and HIPAA/PCI-DSS support.

faqs.question: How does CockroachDB survive data center outages?
faqs.answer: CockroachDB uses the Raft consensus protocol to replicate data across multiple nodes and regions, enabling multi-active availability so the cluster continues serving reads and writes even if a node or an entire region fails.
